title: Analytical Solution for Estimating Transient Vertical Groundwater Flux from Temperature-Depth Profiles

research.question: How can transient vertical groundwater flux be recovered from changing temperature-depth profiles without assuming flux is constant in time?
research.system: One-dimensional downward heat transport through soil driven by conduction, thermal dispersion, surface-temperature forcing, and vertical groundwater advection.
research.inputData: Initial temperature-depth profiles, time-varying surface temperatures, repeated subsurface profiles, thermal properties, two synthetic transient-flux experiments, and a five-day lysimeter infiltration experiment.
research.method: Solve a flexible transient heat equation analytically, fit an average Darcy flux to each profile, interpolate the flux-time curve, and differentiate cumulative flux to recover instantaneous VGF.
research.benchmark: Finite-element temperature profiles, known synthetic Horton-type flux histories, the constant-q approach, Kurylyk and Irvine (2016), and measured lysimeter flux.
research.uncertainty: Normalized sensitivity and differential importance rank model inputs; SEE quantifies flux recovery; thermometer accuracy and a cross-correlation time shift bound field comparison.
research.conclusion: Differentiating the fitted cumulative-flux trajectory recovers transient VGF more accurately than treating each fitted average as instantaneous, while field comparison requires accounting for heat-propagation lag to the sensors.

narrative.throughline: A temperature profile records accumulated heat advection, so transient groundwater flux appears only after the fitted average-flux history is converted back into an instantaneous rate.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that moving groundwater advects heat and that temperatures can be measured at several depths.
narrative.stakes: A constant-flux assumption can substantially underestimate changing infiltration and misread field water balance.
narrative.resolution: The recovered flux hydrograph returns to the lysimeter and agrees with measured infiltration after the sensor-depth propagation lag is made explicit.

background.premise: Vertical groundwater flux varies through time under natural hydrologic forcing.
background.visibleEvidence: Rainfall pulses drive a changing downward flux hydrograph through a soil column.
background.whyNow: This invalidates the constant-flux premise used for analytical tractability.
background.boundary: The paper estimates a depth-averaged one-dimensional vertical flux.
background.sourceExcerpt: changing over time. Consequently, a proper method should be devel­ oped to predict transient VGF.
background.visualObject: Soil column with rainfall-driven transient downward water arrows
background.motionPurpose: Vary arrow length through time while keeping the sensing depths fixed.
background.stepDetail: Apply rainfall; pulse the downward flux; move heat through depth; retain the resulting profile sequence.
background.transitionBridge: The changing arrows become the unknown flux inferred from temperature profiles.

background.premise: Subsurface temperature-depth profiles are practical heat-tracer observations of vertical water movement, and measuring them requires only selected-depth thermometers.
background.visibleEvidence: A borehole cable samples temperature at fixed depths as the thermal front passes.
background.whyNow: This supplies the observable object for the inverse problem.
background.boundary: Heat transport is modeled as one-dimensional downward flow with prescribed thermal properties.
background.sourceExcerpt: Using the TD profiles to estimate the VGF has the benefit that measuring
background.visualObject: Vertical thermometer string intersecting a family of temperature-depth curves
background.motionPurpose: Sample the moving thermal profile at fixed sensor depths.
background.stepDetail: Place sensors; draw the initial profile; propagate heat downward; reveal repeated depth profiles.
background.transitionBridge: The profile family enters the analytical heat model in M03.

background.premise: The VGF in the hydrological cycle is an instantaneous process, whereas a fitted constant q represents a measurement-interval average.
background.visibleEvidence: A changing true hydrograph and one interval-average bar enclose the same cumulative area.
background.whyNow: The distinction explains why an additional derivative is required.
background.boundary: The conversion follows the paper's definition of q as an average over the measurement period.
background.sourceExcerpt: The VGF in the hydrological cycle is always an instantaneous process, so
background.visualObject: Transient flux curve paired with equal-area average-flux bars
background.motionPurpose: Preserve cumulative area while replacing the curve with interval averages.
background.stepDetail: Draw the actual curve; shade cumulative area; replace it with an equal-area bar; mark q as averaged.
background.transitionBridge: The average bars are fitted in M04 and converted in M06.

methods.validity: The cubic form is more flexible than prior linear or exponential initial profiles.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The condition describing the initial temperature profile is expressed
methods.visualObject: Measured initial temperatures aligned by a cubic depth curve
methods.motionPurpose: Bend a cubic curve through the initial sensor points.
methods.stepDetail: Plot initial measurements; adjust cubic coefficients; settle the initial profile.
methods.transitionBridge: The initial curve remains beneath the surface forcing fitted in M02.

methods.validity: A finite trigonometric series represents irregular diurnal and longer surface variations.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the top BC here is regarded as a time-dependent function
methods.visualObject: Irregular surface-temperature trace reconstructed from harmonic components
methods.motionPurpose: Combine periodic components into the measured surface boundary.
methods.stepDetail: Plot surface data; reveal harmonic terms; adjust amplitude period and phase; settle the fitted boundary.
methods.transitionBridge: The boundary and initial profile close the heat equation in M03.

methods.input: One-dimensional heat equation with fitted initial condition and surface boundary
methods.output: Analytical temperature-depth solution for a prescribed average Darcy flux
methods.validity: The linear advection-dispersion equation with infinite-depth zero-gradient boundary admits the Laplace-transform solution.
methods.locator: PDF pages 3-4 and 9-10, Section 2.1, Equation (6), Appendix A
methods.sourceExcerpt: The analytical solution for the temperature distributions in the soils is derived using the Laplace transform method
methods.visualObject: Boundary and initial curves propagating through a vertical soil column into predicted profiles
methods.motionPurpose: Show conduction spreading and advection shifting the thermal signal with depth.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the initial profile; impose surface forcing; propagate conduction; shift by advection; reveal predicted profiles.
methods.transitionBridge: The predicted profile becomes the curve fitted to each observation time in M04.

methods.input: Observed hourly temperature-depth profile and analytical predictions
methods.output: One fitted q value for each observation time
methods.validity: The Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm is used to estimate q by minimizing temperature-profile residuals.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm (Press et al., 1992) to estimate q using the least-squares method.
methods.visualObject: Predicted profile aligning with measured depth temperatures as q changes
methods.motionPurpose: Adjust flux until residual segments at all depths contract.
methods.stepDetail: Place measured points; draw a trial profile; vary q; minimize residuals; store the fitted average.
methods.transitionBridge: The sequence of average values is smoothed in M05.

methods.input: Discrete fitted q values through time
methods.validity: The paper uses cubic-spline interpolation before differentiation.
methods.sourceExcerpt: We adopt the cubic spline method to interpolate the estimated q
methods.visualObject: Discrete average-flux points joined by a smooth spline
methods.motionPurpose: Replace isolated estimates with a smooth trajectory without changing their locations.
methods.stepDetail: Plot q estimates; draw local spline segments; join derivatives continuously; retain the smooth curve.
methods.transitionBridge: The smooth q trajectory becomes cumulative flux q t in M06.

methods.output: Instantaneous actual vertical groundwater flux qa(t)
methods.validity: Equation (7) defines qa as d(qt)/dt because fitted q is time averaged.
methods.sourceExcerpt: qa(t) is derived from d(qt)/dt and expressed as:
methods.visualObject: Tangent slope moving along the cumulative-flux curve and writing the transient hydrograph
methods.motionPurpose: Turn accumulated flux into the instantaneous rate visible at each time.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply q by time; trace cumulative flux; place a tangent; move the tangent; plot its slope as qa.
methods.transitionBridge: The recovered hydrograph is tested against numerical truth in M07.

methods.input: Predicted soil temperature and one perturbed input parameter
methods.output: Normalized temperature sensitivity Xm for each parameter
methods.validity: Equation 9 uses a forward difference with the reported increment of 10^-3 Pm to approximate the normalized derivative.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Kabala (2001) defines the normalized sensitivity as:
methods.visualObject: One perturbed parameter feeding a normalized temperature-response curve
methods.motionPurpose: Convert each finite parameter perturbation into its signed normalized temperature response.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the baseline response, perturb one parameter by 10^-3 Pm, calculate the temperature difference, and scale it by Pm.
methods.transitionBridge: The separate sensitivity magnitudes become inputs to the importance measure in M08.

methods.input: Absolute normalized sensitivities for all model parameters
methods.output: Differential importance measure DIMm for each parameter
methods.validity: Equation 10 divides each absolute sensitivity by the sum of absolute sensitivities across the model parameters.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The DIM for each parameter is defined as:
methods.visualObject: Normalized sensitivity curves converted into fractional importance curves
methods.motionPurpose: Express each parameter's sensitivity as its share of the total absolute response.
methods.stepDetail: Take each absolute Xm curve, divide it by the visible sum across parameters, and retain the resulting DIMm curve.
methods.transitionBridge: The ranked response controls accompany the independent finite-element response generated in M09.

methods.input: Heat equations, boundary conditions, finite-depth approximation, shared parameters, and the reported finite-element mesh
methods.output: Finite-element temperature-depth profiles at the reported comparison times
methods.validity: The finite-element solution uses Equations (2)-(5), a 500 m lower boundary, 500 nodes, and finer meshes near the ground surface.
methods.locator: PDF pages 5-6, Sections 2.3 and 3.1, Figures 3 and 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: the finite-element solution developed in Section 2.3 to generate the TD profiles
methods.visualObject: Finite-element soil column generating temperature-depth profiles at five times
methods.motionPurpose: Produce the independent numerical response before displaying the analytical curves.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the shared conditions, discretize the finite soil column, solve the nodal temperatures, and retain five temporal profiles.
methods.transitionBridge: The generated finite-element profiles join the analytical profiles for comparison in M10.

methods.input: Analytical temperature-depth profiles and generated finite-element profiles at five common times
methods.output: Verification of the analytical forward model
methods.validity: The same parameters and boundary conditions are used; reported departures are below 0.1 degrees C.
methods.sourceExcerpt: present analytical solution are almost in agreement with those yielded
methods.visualObject: Analytical and finite-element temperature-depth curves overlaid at five times
methods.motionPurpose: Overlay the independently generated solutions so their agreement is directly inspectable.
methods.stepDetail: Draw the analytical profiles, add the finite-element profiles, overlay each time, and mark the maximum departure.
methods.transitionBridge: Verified profiles support flux-recovery experiments in M11.

methods.output: SEE comparison between present and constant-q approaches
methods.validity: Known Horton-type input hydrographs provide direct truth for shallow and deep cases.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The present approach yields a flux value more consistent with the true flux than the constant-q approach.
methods.visualObject: True, present-approach, and constant-q hydrograph curves with scalar SEE readouts
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal underestimation by the constant-q curve while retaining the source-defined scalar error comparison.
methods.stepDetail: Plot the three hydrograph curves and display the reported SEE values beside each estimated curve.
methods.transitionBridge: The validated recovery chain is applied only to screened lysimeter profiles in M12.

methods.output: Field inversion profiles from the second through fifth day
methods.validity: The paper retains profiles only after infiltrating water has passed the thermometers so temperature can respond to transient VGF.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 2.6 Field infiltration experiment
methods.sourceExcerpt: TD profiles are selected in saturated stages, from the second to the fifth
methods.visualObject: Five-day profile timeline with days two through five retained
methods.motionPurpose: Screen out the pre-saturation interval before estimating field flux.
methods.stepDetail: Show the five-day record, mark water passage through the fixed sensors, and retain only the saturated-stage profiles.
methods.transitionBridge: The screened profiles produce the estimated field hydrograph whose lag is measured in M13.

methods.input: Estimated and measured lysimeter VGF hydrographs before alignment
methods.output: A same-data cross-correlation lag of about 1.08 days
methods.validity: The maximum cross-correlation is calculated from the same paired hydrographs later aligned for comparison; it is a lag estimate from those data, not a separate validation dataset.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.4 Field application, Figure 9
methods.sourceExcerpt: A maximum cross-correlation method is utilized to quantify the time lag between these two hydrographs
methods.visualObject: Paired hydrographs feeding a cross-correlation peak at 1.08 days
methods.motionPurpose: Estimate the horizontal offset from the two curves without treating it as separate evidence.
methods.stepDetail: Plot both hydrographs, scan their relative offset, and mark the maximum cross-correlation at about 1.08 days.
methods.transitionBridge: The estimated offset becomes the single shift applied in M14.

methods.input: Measured VGF hydrograph and the estimated 1.08-day lag
methods.output: Measured flux curve displaced by 1.08 days
methods.validity: The paper applies the derived time shift to the measured hydrograph before judging curve agreement.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 3.4 Field application, Figure 11
methods.sourceExcerpt: With 1.08 days of time shift in the measured hydrograph
methods.visualObject: Measured hydrograph translated horizontally beside the fixed estimated curve
methods.motionPurpose: Apply the reported lag as a visible horizontal translation only.
methods.stepDetail: Keep the estimated curve fixed and translate the measured curve by 1.08 days along the time axis.
methods.transitionBridge: The shifted curves are assessed for agreement in M15.

methods.input: Shifted measured hydrograph and estimated hydrograph
methods.output: Consistent curve shapes with average fluxes of 4.67 and 4.60 mm per hour and a 0.07 mm per hour difference
methods.validity: The agreement assessment follows the same-data lag adjustment and is reported with curves and scalar averages rather than uncertainty bands.
methods.locator: PDF pages 8-9, Section 3.4 Field application, Figure 11
methods.sourceExcerpt: these two hydrographs seem consistent (see Fig. 11).
methods.visualObject: Shifted measured and estimated curves with scalar average-flux readouts
methods.motionPurpose: Assess post-shift agreement using the reported curve comparison and mean-flux difference.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the shifted curves and display 4.67, 4.60, and their 0.07 mm per hour difference as scalar labels.
methods.transitionBridge: The source-qualified field comparison returns to the lysimeter and sensor depths.

symbols.meaning: Time-averaged Darcy flux fitted over a measurement period
symbols.transition: Transform shaded cumulative area into the average bar
Transform the cumulative-curve tangent into the hydrograph ordinate

symbols.visualObject: Width added to the moving thermal front

return.visualObject: Original lysimeter soil column with fixed thermometers and aligned measured-estimated flux hydrographs
return.claim: The analytical-profile inversion followed by d(qt)/dt provides a practical estimate of transient depth-averaged VGF and improves on constant-q inversion.
return.claimBoundary: The model assumes one-dimensional downward heat transport and known thermal properties; the field comparison requires a 1.08-day heat-propagation shift, and heterogeneity may explain residual sensor-depth differences.
return.locator: PDF pages 8-9, Section 3.4 and Conclusions, Figures 9-11
return.sourceExcerpt: the averaged infiltration rate of the experiment is 4.67 mm/h, while the averaged flux predicted by the present approach is 4.60 mm/h

visual.primaryObject: Vertical soil column with repeated temperature-depth profiles
visual.accentObject: Tangent converting cumulative fitted flux into instantaneous VGF
visual.measuredQuantity: Temperature by depth and transient vertical groundwater flux
visual.geometryNotes: Use depth increasing downward. Keep sensor depths fixed while thermal profiles move. Distinguish fitted average q bars from instantaneous qa(t), show synthetic errors as scalar SEE values, and show the field comparison with paired curves, scalar averages, and the reported horizontal time shift.
